methods.description: Parsimony Analysis of Endemicity (Rosen, 1988;Morrone and Crisci, 1995;Ramírez-Arriaga et al., 2008, 2014) was used to compare the palynomorph diversity of Cuayuca Formation outcrop sections... In hierarchic grouping, sections were clustered according to the presence of taxa.

steps.description: The WINCLADA data matrix from PAE analysis (Nixon, 2002) shows rows that correspond to outcrop sections and columns for palynomorphs, whose presence (1) or absence (0) in each section is indicated.
steps.searchable_summary: Create a presence/absence data matrix for sections and palynomorphs
inputs: Presence/absence data for each palynomorph in each section

steps.description: NONA was used to carry out the parsimony analysis (Goloboff, 1993).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform parsimony analysis using NONA software

steps.description: The PAE analysis included a heuristic search with tree bisection and reconnection (TBR), branch swapping with 500 replications holding 10 trees per replication, and further expanding the memory to hold up to 10,000 trees.
steps.searchable_summary: Configure parsimony analysis parameters (heuristic search, TBR, replications, tree limits, memory)

steps.description: WINCLADA calculated three statistics that reflect the degree of conflict among data: a) Length=L; b) Consistency Index=CI; and c) Retention Index=RI.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate parsimony statistics (Length, Consistency Index, Retention Index)

steps.description: In the present work, clades are supported by the presence of palynomorphs, and unique occurrences are emphasized with black circles.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and interpret clades and unique occurrences based on parsimony analysis results
outputs: Unique palynomorph occurrences associated with clades

methods.description: CONISS analysis was applied to recognize paleopalynological changes throughout Cuayuca Formation Mcy Member. The graph (Figure 8) shows four paleopalynological groups.

inputs: Pollen assemblage data (e.g., species abundance/presence per sample/section)

steps.searchable_summary: Perform CONISS (Constrained Incremental Sum of Squares) analysis

steps.description: to recognize paleopalynological changes throughout Cuayuca Formation Mcy Member. The graph (Figure 8) shows four paleopalynological groups.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify paleopalynological groups based on CONISS clustering

steps.description: Samples taken from the base of the Cuayuca Formation, group A, are characterized by the dominance of Alnus vera, Rhoipites and Tubulifloridites; group B shows dominance of the Momipites group and Tubulifloridites; Momipites group and Liliacidites characterize group C; finally, group D shows abundance of temperate elements such as Momipites group, Ulmipollenites...
steps.searchable_summary: Characterize paleopalynological groups by dominant taxa/elements
outputs: Description of each paleopalynological group (dominant taxa, ecological interpretation)

steps.description: In summary, within the upper sections of the Cuayuca Formation, tropical deciduous forest, and arid tropical scrub, as well as cloud forest and Pinus forest are well represented, contrasting with the lower sections, where Rhoipites and Tubulifloridites elements from tropical deciduous forest are dominant.
steps.searchable_summary: Summarize paleopalynological changes and interpret paleoenvironments across sections based on group distribution
